headline: Australia's Numbat Makes Remarkable Recovery From Brink of Extinction
meta_description: After 40 years of intensive conservation work, Australia's numbat population has rebounded from just 300 to between 2,000 and 3,000 individuals.
summary_short: The striped numbat, Western Australia's emblem, has been downlisted from Endangered to Near Threatened after a four-decade conservation effort that brought it back from…
body:
After more than 40 years of dedicated conservation work, Australia has achieved a major wildlife milestone: the numbat, a striped termite-eating marsupial and Western Australia's animal emblem, has been officially downlisted from Endangered to Near Threatened by the IUCN. The turnaround is remarkable. By the late 1970s, fewer than 300 numbats remained in the wild, their survival hanging by a thread as introduced predators, habitat loss, and changing fire patterns pushed them toward extinction. Today, between 2,000 and 3,000 individuals live across five separate populations spanning three states.

The breakthrough came through a two-pronged strategy. Wildlife teams launched aggressive campaigns to control introduced red foxes and feral cats—the primary killers of numbats. These efforts produced swift, dramatic results. One population in Western Australia now harbors up to 2,000 individuals alone. Simultaneously, conservationists built predator-proof enclosures and relocated numbats, both wild-caught and zoo-bred, to establish new populations. Three populations now thrive in Western Australia, alongside one each in South Australia and New South Wales. The oldest of these newer populations has survived for more than 30 years.

Yet success remains conditional. As Tony Friend, a research associate at the Western Australian Department of Biodiversity, Conservation and Attractions, emphasized: "The effort must continue or the numbat will quickly fall back to low levels or into extinction." Geographic spread is equally crucial—having multiple populations across different regions protects against localized disasters like drought or disease. The conservation teams continue pushing to expand both numbat numbers and their range further. This victory belongs not to a single breakthrough moment, but to the unsexy, unglamorous work of sustained predator management—proving that persistent, determined effort can call species back from oblivion.
quick_facts: Population recovered from 300 to 2,000–3,000 individuals in 40 years
Five populations now established across Western Australia, South Australia, and New South Wales
Controlling introduced foxes and feral cats was the key intervention
One Western Australian population now holds up to 2,000 numbats
Downlisted from Endangered to Near Threatened by IUCN
